sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content:
Genetic predisposition: Familial neuroblastoma cases are rare (estimated to be about 1%–2% of all cases) and have been associated with germline gain-of-function ALK mutations and loss-of-function PHOX2B mutations [12-16]. Other genetic syndromes associated with neuroblastoma include Li-Fraumeni syndrome and those caused by mutations in RAS pathway genes (Costello syndrome, Noonan syndrome, neurofibromatosis) [13].
MYCN amplification: Amplification of the MYCN oncogene is associated with aggressive disease and is the strongest independent prognostic risk factor included in the neuroblastoma risk classification system [47,57,58].
Unfavorable Histology: Classification as Unfavorable Histology (UH) by the International Neuroblastoma Pathology Classification (INPC) is a key prognostic factor, based on age at diagnosis, grade of neuroblastic differentiation, and mitosis-karyorrhexis index (MKI) [39,40].
Segmental Chromosomal Aberrations (SCAs): SCAs, such as loss of 1p or 11q and gain of 17q, are associated with inferior outcomes, particularly in MYCN non-amplified tumors [47,59].
Diploid DNA content (DI = 1): A DNA index (DI) = 1 (diploid) may be associated with aggressive disease, depending on other factors including age [47].
Advanced age at diagnosis: Age ≥18 months at diagnosis is associated with high-risk disease, especially for metastatic (Stage M) tumors [47].
Advanced stage at diagnosis: Distant metastatic disease (INRG Stage M) is a feature of high-risk neuroblastoma, particularly in patients ≥12 months of age [47].

cards.detail: Familial neuroblastoma cases are rare (estimated to be about 1%–2% of all cases) and have been associated with germline gain-of-function ALK mutations and loss-of-function PHOX2B mutations [12-16]. Other genetic syndromes associated with neuroblastoma include Li-Fraumeni syndrome and those caused by mutations in RAS pathway genes (Costello syndrome, Noonan syndrome, neurofibromatosis) [13].
Amplification of the MYCN oncogene is associated with aggressive disease and is the strongest independent prognostic risk factor included in the neuroblastoma risk classification system [47,57,58].

Classification as Unfavorable Histology (UH) by the International Neuroblastoma Pathology Classification (INPC) is a key prognostic factor, based on age at diagnosis, grade of neuroblastic differentiation, and mitosis-karyorrhexis index (MKI) [39,40].
SCAs, such as loss of 1p or 11q and gain of 17q, are associated with inferior outcomes, particularly in MYCN non-amplified tumors [47,59].
A DNA index (DI) = 1 (diploid) may be associated with aggressive disease, depending on other factors including age [47].
Age ≥18 months at diagnosis is associated with high-risk disease, especially for metastatic (Stage M) tumors [47].
Distant metastatic disease (INRG Stage M) is a feature of high-risk neuroblastoma, particularly in patients ≥12 months of age [47].
